era_context: Late 15th century: The Italian Renaissance is in full flourish with major artistic and intellectual achievements; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power under Mehmed II; the Iberian Reconquista is in its final phase; the Ming Dynasty in China is stable and culturally vibrant; the Aztec Empire is expanding under Axayacatl; European maritime exploration is accelerating along the African coast.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pacific Islander societies (no contemporary written record)

events.title: Leonardo da Vinci completes The Last Supper (or in progress)
events.description: Leonardo da Vinci is working on or has recently completed major artistic commissions in Milan in 1480. Leonardo arrived in Milan around 1482, so in 1480 he was likely still in Florence or traveling. However, the year 1480 marks a period of Leonardo's intense artistic and scientific activity. Leonardo's notebooks from this period show his investigations into anatomy, engineering, and art. In 1480, Leonardo was in his late twenties and at the beginning of his mature artistic period. His work during this time laid the foundation for his later masterpieces, including The Last Supper (begun 1495) and the Mona Lisa (begun 1503).

sources.name: Vasari, Lives of the Artists (16th-century biography)

events.certainty_note: Leonardo's exact location and activities in 1480 are not precisely documented. The year is assigned to mark his period of artistic development, though specific works cannot be dated to 1480 with certainty.
cross_references: Part of Leonardo da Vinci's artistic and scientific career

events.title: Ottoman siege of Rhodes
events.description: The Ottoman Empire under Mehmed II launches a major siege of Rhodes in 1480, attempting to capture the island from the Knights Hospitaller. The siege lasted from May to August 1480 and was one of the most significant military engagements of the period. The Ottomans, under the command of Mehmed II, deployed a large fleet and army, but the Knights Hospitaller, led by Grand Master Pierre d'Aubusson, mounted a determined defense. Although the Ottomans inflicted heavy casualties and damage, they ultimately failed to capture the island. The siege of Rhodes demonstrated the military power of the Ottoman Empire and the resilience of the Knights Hospitaller. The failed siege led to negotiations and a peace treaty between the Ottomans and the Knights in 1481.
key_figures: Pierre d'Aubusson (Grand Master of the Knights Hospitaller)
events.certainty_note: The siege of Rhodes in 1480 is well-documented in Ottoman, Hospitaller, and European sources. The dates (May–August 1480) are confirmed by multiple contemporary accounts.
cross_references: Part of Ottoman expansion in the Eastern Mediterranean
Precedes the peace treaty between the Ottomans and the Knights (1481)

events.title: Ivan III consolidates Russian power; end of Mongol overlordship
events.description: Ivan III, known as 'Ivan the Great,' consolidates Russian power and effectively ends Mongol overlordship in 1480. In 1480, Ivan III refused to pay tribute to the Golden Horde, and when the Mongol khan Akhmat attempted to invade Russia, Ivan's forces confronted the Mongols at the Ugra River. The confrontation, known as the 'Standi on the Ugra,' resulted in the Mongols withdrawing without a major battle. This event marked the effective end of Mongol overlordship of Russia, though the formal recognition of Russian independence came later. Ivan III's consolidation of power in 1480 marked the beginning of the rise of Moscow as a major European power and the foundation of the Russian Empire.
events.certainty_note: The 'Standing on the Ugra' in 1480 is well-documented in Russian chronicles. The event is confirmed by multiple sources and marks a watershed moment in Russian history.
cross_references: Part of Ivan III's consolidation of Russian power
Precedes the rise of Moscow as a major European power

events.title: Axayacatl reigns as Aztec Emperor
events.description: Axayacatl (r. 1469–1481) continues his reign as the seventh Aztec emperor in 1480. Axayacatl's reign is characterized by military expansion and the consolidation of Aztec power. In 1480, Axayacatl was in the final year of his reign, overseeing the administration of the Aztec Empire and conducting military campaigns to maintain control over subject city-states. Axayacatl's reign was marked by significant military achievements, including the conquest of the Toluca Valley and the expansion of Aztec tribute networks. Axayacatl's reign would be followed by that of his brother Tizoc (1481–1486).
events.certainty_note: Aztec chronology for this period relies on later indigenous codices and Spanish-era compilations. The exact sequence of events in 1480 is approximate; the broader pattern of Axayacatl's reign is confirmed by multiple sources.

events.title: Chenghua Emperor rules Ming Dynasty
events.description: The Chenghua Emperor (r. 1464–1487) continues his reign over the Ming Dynasty in 1480. The Chenghua Emperor's reign is characterized by relative stability and cultural flourishing. In 1480, the Ming Dynasty was at the height of its power, with a strong bureaucracy, flourishing arts and literature, and extensive trade networks. The Chenghua Emperor's reign saw the continuation of the Great Wall's construction and maintenance, the consolidation of Ming control over China, and the development of Ming ceramics and porcelain that would become world-renowned.

sources.name: Ming Dynasty imperial records and archives

events.certainty_note: The Chenghua Emperor's reign dates are certain.

disconfirming_evidence: Leonardo da Vinci's exact location and activities in 1480 are not precisely documented; the assignment of this year to his artistic development is approximate. The 'Standing on the Ugra' in 1480 is sometimes interpreted as a more symbolic than military confrontation, with some scholars questioning whether a major battle actually occurred. The Aztec chronology for 1480 is approximate and depends on later indigenous sources; no contemporary Aztec written records survive for this specific year.
historiographic_note: 1480 is well-documented in European sources (Ottoman chronicles, Russian chronicles, Italian Renaissance records) but relies on later compilations for non-European regions. Aztec history depends entirely on post-conquest indigenous codices and Spanish-era ethnographic records. Chinese sources are abundant but often focus on court politics. African and Southeast Asian sources are sparse in European archives. The surviving record is heavily weighted toward European, Mediterranean, and East Asian perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Leonardo's artistic development in 1480 led to his masterpiece The Last Supper
The failed siege of Rhodes led to negotiations and a peace treaty in 1481
Ivan III's consolidation of Russian power in 1480 established the foundation for the rise of Moscow
Axayacatl's reign ended with the ascension of his brother Tizoc in 1481
